q: What type of motion is described as an object moving in a circle at constant speed $v$?
In uniform circular motion, what remains constant?
a: Both magnitude and direction of velocity.

q: In uniform circular motion, what continuously changes, even if speed is constant?
a: The radius of the circular path.

q: What does the change in direction of velocity constitute?
An object revolving in a circle is continuously accelerating under what condition?
a: Even when the speed remains constant.
Only when its speed is increasing.
Only when its speed is decreasing.
Only when its direction is constant.

q: How is average acceleration defined?
What happens to instantaneous acceleration when $\Delta t$ approaches zero?
When $\Delta t$ is very small in circular motion, what is the relationship between $\Delta \mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{v}_1$ (or $\mathbf{v}_2$)?
a: $\Delta \mathbf{v}$ is essentially perpendicular to both of them.
$\Delta \mathbf{v}$ is in the same direction as them.

q: In which direction does the acceleration $\mathbf{a}$ point in circular motion when $\Delta t$ is very small?
a: Toward the center of the circle.
Away from the center of the circle.

q: What are the other names for centripetal acceleration?
What is the symbol for centripetal acceleration?
The magnitude of centripetal acceleration $a_c$ can be obtained using the linear speed $v$ and radius $r$ as:
What is $\lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$ in the context of circular motion?
a: The linear speed $v$ of the object.
The angular speed $\omega$ of the object.
The linear acceleration $a$ of the object.
The angular acceleration $\alpha$ of the object.

q: By substituting $v = r \omega$ into the centripetal acceleration formula, what is the alternative expression for $a_c$?
For uniform circular motion, in what direction does the centripetal acceleration vector point?
a: Towards the center of the circular path.
Away from the center of the circular path.

q: For uniform circular motion, what is the direction of the linear velocity vector?
a: Towards the center of the circular path.
Away from the center of the circular path.

q: How are centripetal acceleration and linear velocity related in terms of direction?
a: They are perpendicular to each other at every point in the path.
They are parallel to each other.
They are independent of each other.

q: What characterizes an object moving in nonuniform circular motion?
a: It moves in a circular path with a varying speed (magnitude of velocity).
It moves in a straight line with constant speed.
It moves in a circular path with constant speed.
It moves in an elliptical path.

q: In nonuniform circular motion, what happens to the angular speed of the object?
What does an object experience if its angular speed is changing in nonuniform circular motion?
What does angular acceleration give rise to in nonuniform circular motion?
In nonuniform circular motion, what types of acceleration are present?
a: Tangential acceleration in addition to centripetal acceleration.

q: What is the resultant acceleration of an object in nonuniform circular motion?
a: The vector sum of the centripetal and tangential accelerations.
The scalar sum of the centripetal and tangential accelerations.
The difference between the centripetal and tangential accelerations.
The product of the centripetal and tangential accelerations.

q: What is the vector equation for resultant acceleration $\mathbf{a}$ in nonuniform circular motion?
How are the two acceleration vectors $\mathbf{a}_c$ and $\mathbf{a}_T$ oriented with respect to each other?
a: They are perpendicular to each other.
They are parallel to each other.
They are in the same direction.

q: In which direction is the centripetal acceleration vector $\mathbf{a}_c$?
a: Perpendicular to the plane of rotation.

q: In which direction is the tangential acceleration vector $\mathbf{a}_T$?
a: Perpendicular to the plane of rotation.

q: What is the magnitude of resultant acceleration $a$?
How is the direction of resultant acceleration $\mathbf{a}$ expressed in terms of $\phi$?
Which type of acceleration is present in *both* uniform and nonuniform circular motion?
Which type of acceleration is *unlike* tangential acceleration in its presence in both uniform and nonuniform circular motion?
What does "uniform circular motion" mean according to the Key Words?
a: Motion in a circle at constant speed.
Motion in a circle with varying speed.

q: What does "nonuniform circular motion" mean according to the Key Words?
a: Motion in a circle with varying speed.
Motion in a circle at constant speed.
Rotation at a constant angular velocity.

q: What does "centripetal acceleration" refer to in the Key Words?
a: Acceleration directed towards the center of a circular path.
Acceleration tangent to a circular path.
Acceleration along the axis of rotation.

q: What does "tangential acceleration" refer to in the Key Words?
a: Acceleration component tangent to the circular path.
Acceleration component radial to the circular path.
Acceleration that changes the direction of velocity.

q: What condition implies that angular acceleration is constant, leading to rotational motion under constant angular acceleration?
a: If the angular velocity of the rotating object changes at a constant rate.
If the linear velocity of the rotating object changes at a constant rate.
If the object is at rest.
If the object moves in a straight line.

q: In uniform circular motion, where does the centripetal acceleration vector point?
a: Towards the centre of the circular path.
Away from the centre of the circular path.

q: In uniform circular motion, what is the direction of the linear velocity vector?
a: Towards the centre of the circular path.
Away from the centre of the circular path.
Perpendicular to the plane of motion.

q: What is the name of the acceleration described as pointing towards the center of the circular path with a linear velocity vector tangential to the path in uniform circular motion?
Which of the following describes rotational motion?
a: An object rotates about an axis.
An object just moves in a circle.
An object moves without changing its orientation.
An object translates along a straight line.
